reasoning: The Zimbabwean government announced on April 9, 2025, that it made a $3.1 million cash disbursement to a 'first batch' of 378 farms out of a larger pool of 740 already-approved farms. The 2025 National Budget allocates $10 million for this compensation under the Global Compensation Deed, meaning sufficient funds appear to be set aside to cover the estimated ~$3 million needed for the remaining 362 approved farms. While the fact that the 740 farms are 'approved' and structured into 'batches' strongly implies further payouts are queued up, the timeframe to June 1, 2025 (less than 40 days from today) is quite tight. Historically, sovereign administrative processes in Zimbabwe face significant friction and delays (taking 5 years since the 2020 deal to make this very small first payment). In addition, on April 16, a major dissenting union representing other farmers explicitly rejected the compensation deal, which could stall momentum or pause the administrative rollout. Given the competing pressures of an already-approved pending tranche against typical bureaucratic delays and recent farmer backlash, I assign a 40% probability.
